story:
Arthur's world was all soaring spires and shouted legends; mine was the quiet hum of archives and the steady scratch of a quill. For three years I served as his chronicler, recording his battles and speeches from a respectful distance. He was the sun of the Lumina Prophecy, and I was a faint shadow at the margins. The knights praised his skill, the mages his power, the people his kindness. When they looked at me, they saw only a scribe—part of the furniture in his solar, a footnote in my own life.

The quest for the Sunstone of Atheria was meant to be Arthur's final triumph, the key to ending the creeping Blight. We traveled for over two months with knights and mages. They laughed or argued; I listened and took notes. At the Whispering Peaks, wind tearing at our cloaks, Arthur asked if I marveled. I only felt hollow. "I don't belong here," I whispered. He laughed it off, the chosen one striding ahead.

On the sixty-fifth day, we reached Veridia. Not a shining city, but a ruin swallowed by desert and silence. Magic sputtered. Strength failed. The gates would not yield. The prophecy's "test of a pure heart and a steady hand" had always sounded like combat, yet no weapon would help. For the first time, doubt flickered in Arthur's eyes.

He asked my opinion—on more than phrasing. "I don't know," I said, then spoke the truth that haunted me: "I don't belong here." He bristled. "It's everyone's story." But I knew the shape of the problem: he was lost because this wasn't the tale he understood.

While the camp frayed—knights grumbling, mages bickering, Arthur withdrawing—I did what I'd always done. I read the fragments of Atherian scrolls, studied the city's carvings, walked the walls. The Atherians believed reality was a living story, and the most important stories were written in stillness and silence. I traced geometric patterns in the stone, watched how light fell, heard a faint hum deep within the walls. Then the truth struck: the gate wasn't sealed by force but waiting for resonance—a precise, inner harmony. The "pure heart" was a mind clear of ego; the "steady hand" belonged to someone who did not force, but listened. The prophecy wasn't for a hero. It was for a chronicler.

I told Arthur. He dismissed it as ridiculous. In his frustration, he snapped, "You don't belong here." I stepped forward. "No. I don't belong here"—not as a hero, but as what the moment required. At the gate, I traced the stone's hidden score with careful fingers and breathed in rhythm with the patterns, finding the silent harmony they suggested. The wall answered. The shimmer grew, and the gate dissolved like a ripple in a still pond.

Veridia opened into a silent library, a vast repository of history. At its heart glowed the Sunstone, steady rather than blinding. We returned as heroes. Arthur was celebrated. I wrote the triumph into the Great Compendium, as always, then began a private book: The Unseen Story—the hum of the stones, the fear in Arthur's eyes, the quiet truth beneath the legend.

One rainy afternoon, Arthur left a blank journal on my desk. "This is for you," he said, and went. I opened it and wrote: "My name is Lyra." I looked at the Sunstone's soft light, at my ink-stained hands, at the blank pages. "I don't belong here," I said again—not in the background, not in someone else's myth. I belong where stories are kept honest. I belong to the real story. And I began to write.
